---
title: "Communicate"
subtitle: "Introduction to Quarto"
author: "Morgan Brucks"
institute: "University of Kansas Medical Center"
date: 2026-07-08
image: "../../images/thumbnails/image-template.png"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, "../../styles/styleReveal.scss"]
    slide-number: true
    preview-links: auto
    logo: https://brand.ku.edu/sites/brand/files/styles/wide_col_xl_6/public/images/2020/KUMarksLogo.png
    footer: "R for Lifestyle and Brain Health (R-LAB)"
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: true
    code-block-bg: true
    incremental: false
    date-format: long
    embed-resources: true
execute: 
  freeze: auto
---

## Instructor Notes

This deck is designed for:
- 45-minute lecture
- 45-minute hands-on workshop
- Mixed audience (beginner to advanced researchers)
- Brain health and health sciences examples

::: notes
Key teaching goal: Communication and reproducibility, not memorization of syntax.
Many participants will remember concepts better than commands.
:::


## Opening Question

How many of you have:

- copied a figure from R into PowerPoint?
- copied a table into Word?
- had to recreate a figure months later?
- struggled to remember how results were generated?

::: notes
Spend 2-3 minutes discussing.
This creates buy-in for reproducibility.
:::

---

# Why Communication Matters

R4DS argues that communication is part of data science.

Data science is not:

Data â†’ Analysis â†’ Done

It is:

Data â†’ Analysis â†’ Communication â†’ Impact

::: notes
Ask: "If nobody understands your work, did the analysis accomplish its goal?"
:::

---

## Three Audiences for Quarto

According to R4DS:

1. Decision makers
2. Collaborators
3. Future you

---

## Decision Makers

Need:

- conclusions
- recommendations
- key findings

Usually do NOT need:

- every line of code
- every model output

::: notes
Examples:
Department chairs, grant reviewers, clinicians, leadership.
:::

---

## Collaborators

Need:

- code
- methods
- assumptions
- outputs

Goal: transparency.

---

## Future You

Perhaps the most important audience.

Questions future-you asks:

- What did I do?
- Why did I do it?
- Can I reproduce it?

---

## Brain Health Example

Imagine updating:

- recruitment reports
- cognitive outcomes
- MRI summaries
- grant progress reports

Manual workflow:

- find figure
- recreate figure
- update figure

Reproducible workflow:

- rerun code
- render report

---

# What is Quarto?

Quarto combines:

- narrative
- code
- figures
- tables
- citations

into a single document.

---

# Why Quarto?

Benefits:

- reproducibility
- transparency
- efficiency
- flexibility

---

# One Source, Many Outputs

A single `.qmd` file can create:

- HTML
- PDF
- Word
- presentations
- websites
- books

::: notes
This idea becomes important in Chapter 29.
:::

---

# Anatomy of a Quarto File

Three pieces:

1. YAML
2. Markdown
3. Code chunks

---

# YAML

Think of YAML as settings.

```yaml
---
title: "Brain Health Report"
author: "Morgan Brucks"
format: html
---
```

---

# Markdown

Think of markdown as storytelling.

```markdown
# Results

## Recruitment

**Important finding**
```

---

# Code Chunks

Think of code chunks as analysis.

````markdown

::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
````

---

# Rendering Workflow

```text
.qmd
 â†“
knitr
 â†“
markdown
 â†“
pandoc
 â†“
final output
```

::: notes
Many people click Render for years without understanding this workflow.
:::

---

# Demo #1

Open a blank Quarto document.

Show:

- YAML
- markdown
- chunk
- Render button

::: notes
Switch to RStudio here.
5 minutes.
:::

---

# Visual Editor

Advantages:

- beginner friendly
- Word-like
- point and click

---

# Source Editor

Advantages:

- faster
- more flexible
- easier debugging

---

# Discussion

Which editor would you choose and why?

---

# Markdown Basics

Headers:

```markdown
# Header
## Subheader
### Smaller Header
```

---

# Text Formatting

```markdown
**bold**

*italic*

`code`
```

---

# Lists

```markdown
- item 1
- item 2
```

and

```markdown
1. first
2. second
```

---

# Links

```markdown
[Posit](https://posit.co)
```

---

# Block Quotes

```markdown
> Reproducibility matters.
```

---

# Why Markdown?

Advantages:

- simple
- readable
- portable
- fast

---

# Code Chunks

Basic example:


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     speed           dist       
 Min.   : 4.0   Min.   :  2.00  
 1st Qu.:12.0   1st Qu.: 26.00  
 Median :15.0   Median : 36.00  
 Mean   :15.4   Mean   : 42.98  
 3rd Qu.:19.0   3rd Qu.: 56.00  
 Max.   :25.0   Max.   :120.00  
```


:::
:::


---

---

# Chunk Labels

```r
#| label: demographics-summary
```

Benefits:

- organization
- references
- navigation

---

# Chunk Options

```r
#| echo: false
```

Hide code.

---

# Additional Options

```r
#| warning: false
#| message: false
```

---

# Figure Size

```r
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5
```

---

# Global Options

```r
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE
)
```

---

# Inline Code

Source:

```markdown
There are 32 observations.
```

Result:

There are 32 observations.

---

# Why Inline Code Matters

Examples:

- sample size
- mean age
- enrollment counts
- dates

Automatically updates.

---

::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

Show code/output relationship.
:::

::: {.cell}

:::
:::


---

# Why Figures in Quarto?

Traditional:

- create
- save
- copy
- paste

Quarto:

- update code
- render

---

---

# Research Examples

Possible tables:

- demographics
- recruitment
- cognition outcomes
- intervention adherence

---

# Citations

Chapter 28 introduces references.

YAML:

```yaml
bibliography: references.bib
```

---

# Cross References

Example:

```markdown
See @fig-recruitment.
```

Useful for manuscripts.

---

# Callouts

```markdown
::: {.callout-note}
Important point.
:::
```

---

# Parameters

```yaml
params:
  visit: baseline
```

---

# Why Parameters Matter

Generate:

- participant reports
- visit reports
- study reports

from one template.

---

# Common Errors

- missing package
- YAML spacing
- missing parentheses
- unclosed chunks

---

# Debugging Advice

1. Read the error
2. Find the line
3. Fix one thing
4. Render again

---

# Discussion

What feature seems most useful for your work?

---

# Chapter 29: Formats

Core idea:

One source â†’ many outputs

---

# HTML

```yaml
format: html
```

Best for:

- interactivity
- online sharing

---

# PDF

```yaml
format: pdf
```

Best for:

- printing
- manuscripts

---

# Word

```yaml
format: docx
```

Best for:

- tracked changes
- collaborators

---

# RevealJS

```yaml
format: revealjs
```

Best for:

- teaching
- conference talks
- lab meetings

---

# Multiple Formats

```yaml
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
  docx: default
```

---

# Websites

```yaml
project:
  type: website
```

---

# Books

```yaml
project:
  type: book
```

---

# Brain Health Applications

Potential uses:

- study reports
- MRI summaries
- grant updates
- recruitment tracking
- conference presentations

::: notes
Customize with GETActive examples.
This is where your own examples will shine.
:::

---

# Best Practices

- render often
- keep files organized
- write narrative
- document decisions
- embrace reproducibility

---

# Transition to Workshop

Now let's build a Quarto presentation.

---

# Workshop Activity 1

Create a RevealJS presentation.

Tasks:

- render
- change title
- change theme

---

# Workshop Activity 2

Create slides with:

- heading
- bold text
- italic text
- bullets

---

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

Add a plot.

# Workshop Activity 5

Create:

- title
- plot
- table
- interpretation sentence

---

# Reflection

Discuss:

- What surprised you?
- What seems useful?
- Where could you use Quarto?

---

# Final Takeaways

Quarto helps us:

- communicate
- reproduce
- automate
- collaborate
- publish

---

# Questions

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
